md: A **ratio** compares two quantities. If a recipe calls for 2 cups of flour and 3 cups of sugar, the ratio of flour to sugar is **2 to 3**.
### Three ways to write the same ratio
All three of these mean the same thing:
Ratios look just like fractions — and that's fine, because they obey the same simplification rules.

Multiply or divide *both* parts of a ratio by the same nonzero number, and you get an equivalent ratio. The visualization below shows two ratios as bars — try adjusting the second one until both bars look the same.
All of these describe the same proportion. Just like $\dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{6}{9}$ as fractions.

blocks.title: Simplifying a ratio
blocks.body: Divide both parts by their greatest common divisor. $12 : 8$? $\gcd(12, 8) = 4$, so $12 : 8 = 3 : 2$ in simplest form.

md: **Mix.** A pancake batter uses a $1 : 2$ ratio of flour to milk. If you use $3$ cups of flour, you need $6$ cups of milk.
**Map scale.** A map's scale is $1 : 50000$. Each centimeter on the map is $50000$ centimeters in the real world (i.e., $500$ meters).
**Class.** A class has $10$ girls and $15$ boys. Girls : boys is $10 : 15 = 2 : 3$.

The ratio $2 : 3$ (girls to boys) is **part-to-part**. The total number of students is $2 + 3 = 5$ parts. So $\tfrac{2}{5}$ of the class is girls (a **part-to-whole** ratio). Both are useful, but they're different things.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: A ratio compares two quantities. Three ways to write it: $a : b$, '$a$ to $b$', $\tfrac{a}{b}$. Multiply or divide both parts by the same number for equivalent ratios. Simplify by dividing by the GCD. Watch the difference between part-to-part and part-to-whole.
